questions.specification:
The temperature of a cup of tea, $H$ $^\circ\text{C}$, is modelled by the function $H(t) = P(r^t) + Q$, where $t$ is the time in minutes after the tea is served. The graph of $H$ is shown in the diagram. The graph has a horizontal asymptote at $H = 22$. The graph passes through the point $(0, 70)$. After 2 minutes, the temperature of the tea is $34$ $^\circ\text{C}$.

![Graph of H(t)](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-21de7c7b-01ed-45ca-91aa-999ac140267e-1771929956947/b9900b3f-2209-4255-b0cd-3bdeafcc98b0.jpeg)

parts.content: Write down the value of $Q$.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the horizontal asymptote from the graph or description: $H = 22$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **State** the value: $Q = 22$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Find the value of $P$.
parts.markscheme:
- **Substitute** the initial point $(0, 70)$ and $Q=22$ into the model: $70 = P(r^0) + 22$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Solve** for $P$: $P = 70 - 22 = 48$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Find the value of $r$.
parts.markscheme:
- **Substitute** the point $(2, 34)$ and $P=48, Q=22$ into the model: $34 = 48(r^2) + 22$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Solve** for $r$: $12 = 48r^2 \Rightarrow r^2 = 0.25 \Rightarrow r = 0.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: The tea is at the perfect temperature to drink when $H = 28$. Find the time $t$ when this occurs.
parts.markscheme:
- **Set up** the equation using the found parameters: $28 = 48(0.5^t) + 22$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Solve** for $t$: $6 = 48(0.5^t) \Rightarrow 0.125 = 0.5^t \Rightarrow t = 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

An engineer is modeling the cross-section of a mound of earth discovered during a construction project, represented by the shaded region $R$. The cross-section is shown in the following diagram, where the $x$-axis represents the horizontal distance from a vertical retaining wall and the $h$-axis represents the height, both measured in metres.

The line segments $[AB]$, $[CD]$, and $[AD]$ represent the left boundary, right boundary, and the ground level, respectively. The function $h(x)$ models the upper surface of the mound between points $B$ and $C$ and is given by $h(x) = -0.04x^2 + 1.2x + 10$, for $0 \le x \le 30$.

parts.content: A radioactive substance decays exponentially. Initially there are 100 grams of the substance. Determine the decay constant and find the time at which $f(x) = 50$, indicating when half of the substance has decayed.
parts.markscheme:
### Method #1

- State the function: $f(x) = 100e^{-0.5x}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute $f(x) = 50$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
50 &= 100e^{-0.5x} \\
e^{-0.5x} &= 0.5 \\
-0.5x &= \ln(0.5) \\
x &= \frac{\ln(0.5)}{-0.5} = 1.39
\end{aligned}
$$
<marks>A1</marks>

Therefore, the decay constant is $0.5$ and the time when $f(x) = 50$ (half-life) is $x = 1.39$.

questions.specification:
A landscape architect is designing a parabolic garden feature, represented by the function
$f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 3$

This function describes the height of the garden feature at different horizontal positions $x$.

parts.content: Identify the vertex of the function and determine the range of heights for the garden feature.
parts.markscheme:
The function is in vertex form $f(x) = a(x - h)^2 + k$, where $(h, k)$ is the vertex.

Identify the vertex as $(2, -3)$. <marks>A1</marks>

Determine the range of the function.
Since the parabola opens upwards ($a > 0$), the minimum value is the $y$-coordinate of the vertex.
Range: $y \ge -3$ (or $[-3, \infty)$). <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the vertex form
steps.content:
The given function $f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 3$ is already written in **vertex form**. The general equation for vertex form is:

$$f(x) = a(x - h)^2 + k$$

In this form, the point $(h, k)$ represents the vertex of the parabola. This is covered in **Topic 2.5: Modelling functions** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Extract the vertex
steps.content:
By comparing our specific function to the general form, we can identify the parameters $h$ and $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 1(x - \mathbf{2})^2 + (\mathbf{-3}) \\ h &= 2 \\ k &= -3 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the vertex of the function is $(2, -3)$. In the context of the garden, this means at a horizontal position of $2$, the height is at its lowest point, $-3$.

steps.title: Determine the opening direction
steps.content:
To find the range, we need to know if the parabola opens upwards or downwards. We look at the value of $a$ (the coefficient in front of the brackets).

Since $a = 1$, which is a positive number ($a > 0$), the parabola opens **upwards**. This confirms that the vertex $y$-coordinate ($k = -3$) is the **minimum** value of the function.

steps.title: State the range
steps.content:
Because the minimum height is $-3$ and the function continues upwards infinitely, the range consists of all values greater than or equal to $-3$.

Range: $y \ge -3$ 
(This can also be written in interval notation as $[-3, \infty)$).

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: You can easily verify the vertex and the lowest point of the graph using your GDC. This is a great way to double-check your work during an exam!

gdc.instructions:

2. Enter the function f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 3.
3. Use the 'G-Solv' (F5) tool and select 'MIN' (F3) to find the minimum point.

4. Follow the prompts for 'Left Bound', 'Right Bound', and 'Guess' near the bottom of the curve.

2. Click on the gray point at the bottom of the parabola to see the coordinates of the vertex.

calculator.description: Graph the function to find the minimum point (vertex).

steps.title: Expand to standard form
steps.content:
To use the axis of symmetry method, we first need to expand the function $f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 3$ into the standard quadratic form $ax^2 + bx + c$.

Starting with the squared term:

$$(x - 2)^2 = (x - 2)(x - 2) = x^2 - 4x + 4$$

Now, subtract the constant 3:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= x^2 - 4x + 4 - 3 \\ f(x) &= x^2 - 4x + 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the coefficients
steps.content:
From our expanded function $f(x) = 1x^2 - 4x + 1$, we can identify the coefficients needed for our formulas:

- $a = 1$
- $b = -4$
- $c = 1$

steps.title: Find the axis of symmetry
steps.content:
The $x$-coordinate of the vertex lies on the axis of symmetry. We use the formula from the data booklet:

$$x = -\frac{b}{2a}$$

Substituting our values:
$$x = -\frac{-4}{2(1)} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$

steps.title: Calculate the vertex y-coordinate
steps.content:
To find the height at the vertex, substitute $x = 2$ back into the original function:

$$\begin{aligned} f(2) &= (2 - 2)^2 - 3 \\ &= 0^2 - 3 \\ &= -3 \end{aligned}$$

So, the vertex of the garden feature is at $(2, -3)$. This marks the lowest point of the parabola.

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
Since the lead coefficient $a = 1$ is positive, the parabola opens upwards. This means the $y$-coordinate of our vertex is the **minimum** height of the garden feature.

The range includes all heights from this minimum value upwards:
$$\text{Range: } y \ge -3 \text{ or } [-3, \infty)$$

In the context of the problem, the garden feature starts at a height of $-3$ and goes up infinitely.

highlights.text: Identify the vertex of the function

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
The range refers to all possible $y$-values (heights) that the function can take. 

Looking at our graph, the lowest point is at $y = -3$. Since the parabola opens upwards forever, the height of the garden feature starts at $-3$ and includes every value above it. 

Therefore, the range is:
$$y \ge -3 \text{ or } [-3, \infty)$$

This satisfies the second part of the question regarding the range of heights.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks for both identifying the vertex correctly and stating the range using proper notation.

- **Vertex:** $(2, -3)$
- **Range:** $y \ge -3$

You have correctly identified the key features of the parabolic model using the GDC analysis method!

steps.title: Expand the function
steps.content:
To prepare the function for differentiation using the power rule, let's first expand the expression for $f(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= (x - 2)^2 - 3 \\ f(x) &= (x^2 - 4x + 4) - 3 \\ f(x) &= x^2 - 4x + 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.content:
We use the power rule from the calculus section of the data booklet to find the derivative, $f'(x)$. This represents the gradient of the function at any point $x$.

$$f'(x) = 2x - 4$$

steps.title: Find the x-coordinate
steps.content:
The vertex of a parabola is a stationary point where the gradient is zero. We set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x - 4 &= 0 \\ 2x &= 4 \\ x &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the y-coordinate
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $x = 2$ back into the original function to find the height ($y$-coordinate) of the vertex:

$$\begin{aligned} f(2) &= (2 - 2)^2 - 3 \\ f(2) &= 0^2 - 3 \\ f(2) &= -3 \end{aligned}$$

So, the vertex is at $(2, -3)$. This earns you the first mark <marks>A1</marks>.

highlights.text: Identify the vertex of the function

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
Since the coefficient of $x^2$ is positive ($a=1$), the parabola opens upwards. This means the vertex is a **minimum point**.

The minimum height is $-3$, so the garden feature can take any value from $-3$ upwards.

Range: $y \ge -3$ (or $[-3, \infty)$). This earns you the second mark <marks>A1</marks>.

steps.title: Identify the vertex
steps.content:
The function is given in the vertex form $$f(x) = a(x - h)^2 + k$$, where the point $(h, k)$ is the vertex of the parabola. 

Looking at our function:
$$f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 3$$

We can see that $h = 2$ and $k = -3$. Therefore, the vertex is at **$(2, -3)$**. This is the lowest point of our garden feature because the coefficient $a$ is positive ($1$), meaning the parabola opens upwards.

steps.title: Find the $y$-intercept
steps.content:
To find where the graph crosses the $y$-axis, we set $x = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} f(0) &= (0 - 2)^2 - 3 \\ &= (-2)^2 - 3 \\ &= 4 - 3 \\ &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

The $y$-intercept is at **$(0, 1)$**.

steps.title: Find the $x$-intercepts
steps.content:
To find the $x$-intercepts, we set the height to zero, $f(x) = 0$, and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= (x - 2)^2 - 3 \\ 3 &= (x - 2)^2 \\ \pm\sqrt{3} &= x - 2 \\ x &= 2 \pm \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find the approximate values:
- $x_1 = 2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.27$
- $x_2 = 2 + \sqrt{3} \approx 3.73$

The $x$-intercepts are at approximately **$(0.27, 0)$** and **$(3.73, 0)$**.

steps.title: Verify with a calculator
steps.content: You can use your GDC to verify these key points and visualize the shape before sketching.

gdc.instructions:
1. Enter the Graph menu and type in y1 = (x - 2)^2 - 3.
2. Press [F6] (DRAW).
3. Use [F5] (G-Solv) and select 'MIN' for the vertex, 'ROOT' for x-intercepts, and 'Y-ICEPT' for the y-intercept.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = (X - 2)^2 - 3.
2. Press [GRAPH].
3. To find the vertex, press [2nd][TRACE] (CALC), select 'minimum', and follow the prompts.
4. To find intercepts, use [2nd][TRACE] 'zero' for x-intercepts or [2nd][TRACE] 'value' with X=0 for the y-intercept.

2. Click on the gray dots that appear on the curve to see the coordinates for the vertex and intercepts.

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content:
Now, combine all the features to create your sketch:
1. Draw a smooth, U-shaped curve opening upwards.
2. Plot and label the **vertex at $(2, -3)$**.
3. Plot and label the **$y$-intercept at $(0, 1)$**.
4. Plot and label the **$x$-intercepts at $(0.27, 0)$ and $(3.73, 0)$**.

Following the markscheme, you earn **A1** for the correct upward-opening shape, **A1** for labeling the vertex, and **A1** for labeling the intercepts correctly.

highlights.text: y-intercept at (0, 1) and x-intercepts at (2 - \sqrt{3}, 0) and (2 + \sqrt{3}, 0)

steps.title: Enter the function
steps.content:
To start, we need to enter the quadratic function into our graphing calculator. This function represents a parabola, and we are looking for its key features: the vertex (the lowest point, since the coefficient of $x^2$ is positive) and the points where it crosses the axes.

$$f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 3$$

gdc.instructions: In Graph mode, enter (x-2)^2-3 into Y1. Press [DRAW].

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=], then enter (X-2)²-3 into Y1. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type the function into the input bar.

calculator.description: Enter the function into the graphing list to view the parabola.

steps.title: Find the vertex
steps.content:
The vertex is the minimum point of this parabola. We can use the calculator's analysis tools to find the exact coordinates. From the vertex form $(x-h)^2 + k$, we expect the vertex to be at $(h, k)$. Using the calculator confirms this is the lowest point.

$$\text{Vertex} = (2, -3)$$ 

This earns the first mark for clear labeling.

gdc.instructions: Press [F5] (G-Solve), then [F3] (MIN).

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [CALC], select 'minimum'. Choose a point to the left and right of the bottom, then press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Click on the lowest point of the parabola to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the minimum point of the graph.

steps.title: Find the intercepts
steps.content:
Next, we find the $y$-intercept by evaluating the function at $x=0$, and the $x$-intercepts (roots) where the function equals zero. 

1. **$y$-intercept**: When $x=0$, $f(0) = (0-2)^2 - 3 = 1$. The point is $(0, 1)$.
2. **$x$-intercepts**: Using the GDC's root tool, we find:
   - $x \approx 0.268$ (which is $2 - \sqrt{3}$)
   - $x \approx 3.73$ (which is $2 + \sqrt{3}$)

These points are essential for our sketch.

gdc.instructions: To find roots: [F5] (G-Solve) -> [F1] (ROOT). To find y-intercept: [F5] (G-Solve) -> [F4] (Y-ICEPT).

ti84.instructions: To find roots: [2nd] [CALC] -> 'zero'. To find y-intercept: [2nd] [CALC] -> 'value' and enter X=0.

desmos.instructions: Click on the points where the graph crosses the x-axis and y-axis.

calculator.description: Locate the x-intercepts (roots) and the y-intercept.

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content:
Finally, we draw the graph on a set of axes. Make sure to:
1. Draw a smooth, symmetric U-shaped curve (parabola opening upwards).
2. Mark the **vertex** at $(2, -3)$.
3. Mark the **y-intercept** at $(0, 1)$.
4. Mark the **x-intercepts** at approximately $0.27$ and $3.73$.

According to the markscheme, the labels are key to getting full credit!

steps.title: Expand to standard form
steps.content:
To find the $y$-intercept and vertex easily, we first expand the function $f(x) = (x - 2)^2 - 3$ into the standard quadratic form $ax^2 + bx + c$.

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= (x - 2)(x - 2) - 3 \\ &= (x^2 - 4x + 4) - 3 \\ &= x^2 - 4x + 1 \end{aligned}$$

Now we have $a = 1$, $b = -4$, and $c = 1$.

steps.title: Find the $y$-intercept
steps.content:
In the standard form $y = ax^2 + bx + c$, the $y$-intercept is always the constant term $c$, because at the $y$-intercept, $x = 0$.

$$f(0) = 0^2 - 4(0) + 1 = 1$$

So, the $y$-intercept is at **$(0, 1)$**.

steps.title: Locate the vertex
steps.content:
Next, we find the $x$-coordinate of the vertex using the axis of symmetry formula from the data booklet:

$$x = -\frac{b}{2a}$$

Substituting our values $a = 1$ and $b = -4$:

$$x = -\frac{-4}{2(1)} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$

To find the $y$-coordinate, substitute $x = 2$ back into the function:

$$f(2) = (2 - 2)^2 - 3 = -3$$

So, the vertex is at **$(2, -3)$**.

steps.title: Find the $x$-intercepts
steps.content:
To find where the graph crosses the $x$-axis, we set $f(x) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} (x - 2)^2 - 3 &= 0 \\ (x - 2)^2 &= 3 \\ x - 2 &= \pm\sqrt{3} \\ x &= 2 \pm \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, $2 - \sqrt{3} \approx 0.27$ and $2 + \sqrt{3} \approx 3.73$.

The $x$-intercepts are at **$(0.27, 0)$** and **$(3.73, 0)$**.

gdc.instructions: Use the root or zero function to find where the graph crosses the x-axis.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 - √(3) and 2 + √(3) on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Graph y = (x-2)^2 - 3 and click on the points where the curve crosses the x-axis.

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical values of the x-intercepts.

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content:
Finally, sketch the parabola. Since $a = 1$ (positive), it opens upwards. 

Ensure you clearly label:
1. The **vertex** at $(2, -3)$.
2. The **$y$-intercept** at $(0, 1)$.
3. The **$x$-intercepts** at $(0.27, 0)$ and $(3.73, 0)$.

Marks are awarded for the correct U-shape ($A1$), labeling the vertex ($A1$), and labeling the intercepts ($A1$).

steps.title: Define intercepts
steps.content:
To find where a function crosses the axes, we look for two things:
1. The **x-intercept**: This occurs when the output of the function is zero ($y = 0$).
2. The **y-intercept**: This occurs when the input of the function is zero ($x = 0$).

This falls under **Topic 2: Functions** (specifically SL 2.4). Understanding how to set specific variables to zero is a key skill for analyzing any graph.

highlights.text: Calculate the $x$- and $y$-intercepts of the graph of $f(x)$

steps.title: Calculate the x-intercept
steps.content:
To find the x-intercept, we set the function $f(x)$ equal to zero:

$$\frac{2x + 1}{x - 3} = 0$$

For a rational function (a fraction) to be equal to zero, its **numerator** must be equal to zero. We solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + 1 &= 0 \\ 2x &= -1 \\ x &= -\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, you would earn **[1 mark]** for showing this calculation and arriving at $x = -0.5$.

gdc.instructions: Use the Equation Solver tool. Enter the equation 2x + 1 = 0 and solve for x.

ti84.instructions: Go to [MATH] -> [SOLVE] (or use the solver in later versions). Enter 2X + 1 = 0 and solve for X.

desmos.instructions: Type the equation y = (2x+1)/(x-3). Click on the point where the graph crosses the x-axis to find the intercept.

calculator.description: Solve for x by setting the numerator to zero.

steps.title: Calculate the y-intercept
steps.content:
To find the y-intercept, we substitute $x = 0$ into the function. This tells us the value of the function as it crosses the vertical axis:

$$\begin{aligned} f(0) &= \frac{2(0) + 1}{0 - 3} \\ f(0) &= \frac{1}{-3} \\ f(0) &= -\frac{1}{3} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you the final **[1 mark]**. It is often written as the point $(0, -\frac{1}{3})$ or simply the value $y = -\frac{1}{3}$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression (2*0 + 1) / (0 - 3) in the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: On the home screen, type (2*0 + 1) / (0 - 3) and press [ENTER]. To see as a fraction, press [MATH] -> [1: >Frac].

desmos.instructions: Type f(0) after defining the function f(x) = (2x+1)/(x-3), or click the intercept on the y-axis.

calculator.description: Evaluate the function at x = 0.

steps.title: Understand the Goal
steps.content:
To find the intercepts of the function $f(x) = \frac{2x + 1}{x - 3}$ using a graphical approach, we need to locate where the graph crosses the axes:

1.  **$x$-intercept**: The point where the graph crosses the horizontal axis ($y = 0$).
2.  **$y$-intercept**: The point where the graph crosses the vertical axis ($x = 0$).

We will use the built-in analysis tools on your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC) to find these points precisely.

Once entered, press **Graph**. You may need to adjust your window (Zoom Standard is usually a good start) to see where the graph crosses the axes.

steps.title: Find the y-intercept
steps.content:
The $y$-intercept occurs exactly when $x = 0$. 

Instead of searching the graph, we can use the 'Value' or 'Trace' tool to tell the calculator to look at $x = 0$ specifically.

The calculator will display:
$$y = -0.333333...$$

In IB exams, we usually write this as a fraction or to 3 significant figures. Since $-0.333...$ is exactly $-\frac{1}{3}$, that is our answer.

Based on our graphical analysis, we have found the two intercepts required by the question:

- **$x$-intercept**: $x = -0.5$ (or $-\frac{1}{2}$)
- **$y$-intercept**: $y = -0.333$ (or $-\frac{1}{3}$)

Each of these correctly identifies the coordinates where the graph meets the axes.

steps.title: Understand the function components
steps.content:
To find the asymptotes of a rational function like $f(x) = \frac{2x + 1}{x - 3}$, we need to examine two things: where the function is undefined (vertical) and how it behaves as $x$ gets very large (horizontal).

This falls under **Topic 2: Functions** in your syllabus. Understanding the features of a graph is essential for interpreting models.

steps.title: Find the vertical asymptote
steps.content:
The vertical asymptote occurs where the function is undefined, which happens when the denominator equals zero.

Set the denominator to zero and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x - 3 &= 0 \\ x &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

So, the vertical asymptote is the line $x = 3$.

steps.title: Find the horizontal asymptote
steps.content:
For rational functions where the degree of the numerator and denominator are the same (both are degree 1 here), the horizontal asymptote is found by taking the ratio of the **leading coefficients**.

In our function:
- The leading coefficient of the numerator ($2x + 1$) is $2$.
- The leading coefficient of the denominator ($x - 3$) is $1$.

The horizontal asymptote is:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= \frac{2}{1} \\ y &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify using technology
steps.content: In the IB AI course, you are encouraged to use your Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to verify these features by graphing the function and looking for the lines the curve approaches but never touches.

gdc.instructions:

2. Enter the function: f1(x) = (2x + 1) / (x - 3).
3. Draw the graph and observe the vertical break at x = 3 and the horizontal leveling at y = 2.

2. Press [GRAPH].
3. Use [TRACE] to see that as X approaches 3, Y becomes very large.
4. Use [TABLE] (2nd -> GRAPH) to see the error at X = 3.

2. Type x = 3 to see the vertical line.
3. Type y = 2 to see the horizontal line.

calculator.description: Graph the function to visualize the asymptotes.

steps.title: State the final equations
steps.content:
Based on our calculations:
- Vertical Asymptote: $x = 3$
- Horizontal Asymptote: $y = 2$

In an exam, simply stating these equations clearly will earn you both marks.

steps.title: Understand the transformation goal
steps.content:
To find the asymptotes easily, we want to rewrite the function $f(x) = \frac{2x + 1}{x - 3}$ in the form:

$$f(x) = \frac{a}{x - h} + k$$

In this form:
- The **vertical asymptote** is the line $x = h$ (the value that makes the denominator zero).
- The **horizontal asymptote** is the line $y = k$ (the value the function approaches as $x$ gets very large).

steps.title: Manipulate the numerator
steps.content:
We need to rewrite the numerator $2x + 1$ so that it includes a factor of the denominator, $(x - 3)$. 

We know that $2(x - 3) = 2x - 6$. To get from $2x - 6$ back to $2x + 1$, we need to add $7$. So:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= \frac{2(x - 3) + 7}{x - 3} \\ &= \frac{2(x - 3)}{x - 3} + \frac{7}{x - 3} \\ &= 2 + \frac{7}{x - 3} \end{aligned}$$

Now the function is in the form $f(x) = \frac{7}{x - 3} + 2$.

steps.title: Identify the asymptotes
steps.content:
Using our transformed equation $f(x) = \frac{7}{x - 3} + 2$, we can identify the key features:

1.  **Vertical Asymptote:** The denominator is zero when $x - 3 = 0$, so the vertical asymptote is **$x = 3$**.
2.  **Horizontal Asymptote:** As $x$ becomes very large, the fraction $\frac{7}{x - 3}$ approaches $0$. This leaves us with $f(x) \approx 2$. Thus, the horizontal asymptote is **$y = 2$**.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Input f1(x) = (2x + 1) / (x - 3).
3. Input f2(x) = 2.
4. View the graph and look for the vertical break at x = 3 and the horizontal approach to y = 2.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = (2X + 1) / (X - 3).
2. Enter Y2 = 2 for the horizontal asymptote.
3. Press [GRAPH].
4. To see the vertical asymptote behavior, press [2nd] [GRAPH] for the TABLE and observe the error at X = 3.

2. Type x = 3 into the second box (vertical line).
3. Type y = 2 into the third box (horizontal line).

steps.content:
The equations of the asymptotes are:
- Vertical: $x = 3$
- Horizontal: $y = 2$

steps.title: Graph the function
steps.content:
To find the asymptotes using a GDC, start by graphing the function. 

$$f(x) = \frac{2x + 1}{x - 3}$$

Notice that the function looks like it "breaks" or jumps at a certain $x$-value, and flattens out as $x$ moves far to the left or right. This behavior helps us visually identify the asymptotes.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Graphing menu and enter the expression (2x+1)/(x-3).

steps.title: Find the vertical asymptote
steps.content:
A **vertical asymptote** occurs where the function is undefined. On your graph, this is where the curve shoots up toward $+\infty$ or down toward $-\infty$. 

By looking at the graph or using the **Table** feature, you can see that at $x = 3$, the $y$-value is listed as 'ERROR' or undefined. As $x$ gets very close to 3 (like $2.99$ or $3.01$), the $y$-values become extremely large in magnitude. 

This confirms the vertical asymptote is:
$$x = 3$$

gdc.instructions: Open the table view and look for the x-value where y is undefined.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [GRAPH] (TABLE) and scroll to x = 3 to see the error.

desmos.instructions: Click the gear icon and select the table icon. Enter x = 3.

calculator.description: Check the table to find where the function is undefined.

steps.title: Find the horizontal asymptote
steps.content:
A **horizontal asymptote** describes the behavior of the function as $x$ approaches $\infty$ or $-\infty$. 

Use the **Trace** feature or the **Table** to look at very large values of $x$. For example, if you check $x = 1000$, you will see that $y \approx 2.007$. If you check $x = -1000$, $y \approx 1.993$. 

As $x$ grows larger, the function value gets closer and closer to $2$. This tells us the horizontal asymptote is:
$$y = 2$$

desmos.instructions: In the table, add x = 1000 and x = -1000 to see the y-values approach 2.

steps.title: Final Equations
steps.content:
Based on our GDC analysis, we have identified the two asymptotes:

- **Vertical Asymptote:** $x = 3$
- **Horizontal Asymptote:** $y = 2$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for each correctly identified equation. Remember to always write them in the form $x = c$ and $y = k$.

steps.title: Find the vertical asymptote
steps.content:
To find the vertical asymptote of a rational function, we look for the value of $x$ that makes the denominator equal to zero, as the function is undefined there.

$$\begin{aligned} x - 3 &= 0 \\ x &= 3 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us a vertical dashed line at $x = 3$. This is a key feature of the graph, and identifying it correctly earns one mark in the IB markscheme.

steps.title: Find the horizontal asymptote
steps.content:
For a rational function of the form $f(x) = \frac{ax + b}{cx + d}$, the horizontal asymptote is found by comparing the leading coefficients (the coefficients of the $x$ terms).

As $x$ becomes very large ($x \to \infty$), the constants $+1$ and $-3$ become insignificant, and the function behaves like:

$$y = \frac{2x}{x} = 2$$

So, the horizontal asymptote is the horizontal dashed line $y = 2$.

steps.title: Calculate the axes intercepts
steps.content:
Next, we find where the graph crosses the $x$ and $y$ axes.

**For the $y$-intercept (set $x = 0$):**

$$f(0) = \frac{2(0) + 1}{0 - 3} = \frac{1}{-3} = -\frac{1}{3}$$

So, the $y$-intercept is $(0, -\frac{1}{3})$.

**For the $x$-intercept (set $f(x) = 0$):**

A fraction is zero only when its numerator is zero:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + 1 &= 0 \\ 2x &= -1 \\ x &= -\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

So, the $x$-intercept is $(-\frac{1}{2}, 0)$.

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: Before sketching, it's always a good idea to use your GDC to visualize the function. This helps ensure you've placed the branches in the correct quadrants relative to the asymptotes.

gdc.instructions:

steps.content:
To finish, combine all features on a single coordinate plane:
1. Draw the **asymptotes** as dashed lines ($x=3$ and $y=2$).
2. Plot the **intercepts** $(-\frac{1}{2}, 0)$ and $(0, -\frac{1}{3})$.
3. Draw the two **branches** of the hyperbola. One branch passes through the intercepts in the bottom-left region, and the other branch is in the top-right region.

Ensure your curves approach the asymptotes but do not touch or cross them. This approach covers all requirements for the 4 marks available.

steps.title: Input the function
steps.content:
To start, we need to enter the function into your graphing calculator. 

$$f(x) = \frac{2x + 1}{x - 3}$$

When typing this into your GDC, it is crucial to use **parentheses** around both the numerator and the denominator so the calculator follows the correct order of operations.

steps.title: Identify the intercepts
steps.content:
Next, we use the calculator's built-in tools to find where the graph crosses the axes. These are essential 'anchor points' for your sketch.

*   **$x$-intercept**: Use the 'Zero' tool. The calculator finds where $y=0$. We find $x = -0.5$.
*   **$y$-intercept**: Use the 'Value' tool at $x=0$. The calculator shows $y = -0.333$ (which is $-\frac{1}{3}$).

These points correspond to the coordinates $(-\frac{1}{2}, 0)$ and $(0, -\frac{1}{3})$ as required by the markscheme.

steps.title: Locate the asymptotes
steps.content:
Asymptotes are lines that the graph approaches but never reaches. We can identify these by looking at the behavior of the values in the calculator's **Table**:

1.  **Vertical Asymptote**: Notice in the table that at $x = 3$, the calculator displays an error or 'undef'. As $x$ gets very close to 3, the $y$-values become extremely large (positive or negative). This confirms the vertical asymptote is $x = 3$.
2.  **Horizontal Asymptote**: Scroll through the table for very large positive or negative values of $x$. You will see the $y$-values getting closer and closer to 2. This confirms the horizontal asymptote is $y = 2$.

steps.title: Sketch the final graph
steps.content:
Now, translate what you see on your GDC screen onto paper. To earn all 4 marks, your sketch must include:

1.  **Two distinct branches**: One in the top-right region and one in the bottom-left region relative to the asymptotes.
2.  **Labelled Asymptotes**: Draw dashed lines at $x = 3$ and $y = 2$.
3.  **Labelled Intercepts**: Mark and label the points $(-\frac{1}{2}, 0)$ and $(0, -\frac{1}{3})$.

Ensure the curves approach the asymptotes smoothly without crossing or curling away from them. This aligns with **Topic 2.4** and **2.5** of your syllabus regarding features of models.

steps.title: Rewrite in transformation form
steps.content:
To see how the graph of $f(x)$ is transformed from the parent function $y = \frac{1}{x}$, we rewrite the expression using algebraic division or manipulation into the form $f(x) = \frac{a}{x-h} + k$. 

Starting with the given function:

$$f(x) = \frac{2x + 1}{x - 3}$$

We can manipulate the numerator to include a multiple of the denominator:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + 1 &= 2(x - 3) + 6 + 1 \\ &= 2(x - 3) + 7 \end{aligned}$$

Now, substitute this back into the fraction:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= \frac{2(x - 3) + 7}{x - 3} \\ &= \frac{2(x - 3)}{x - 3} + \frac{7}{x - 3} \\ &= 2 + \frac{7}{x - 3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the asymptotes
steps.content:
From the form $f(x) = \frac{7}{x - 3} + 2$, we can identify the horizontal and vertical translations:

1.  **Vertical Asymptote**: The denominator $x - 3$ tells us the graph is shifted 3 units to the right. The function is undefined when the denominator is zero.
    $$x - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3$$
2.  **Horizontal Asymptote**: The constant $+2$ tells us the graph is shifted 2 units up. As $x$ becomes very large, the term $\frac{7}{x-3}$ approaches zero, so $y$ approaches 2.
    $$y = 2$$

steps.title: Calculate the intercepts
steps.content:
To anchor our sketch, we find where the graph crosses the axes:

*   **$y$-intercept**: Set $x = 0$. $f(0) = \frac{2(0) + 1}{0 - 3} = -\frac{1}{3} \approx -0.333$
*   **$x$-intercept**: Set $f(x) = 0$. $0 = \frac{2x + 1}{x - 3} \Rightarrow 2x + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow x = -\frac{1}{2} = -0.5$

steps.content:
Using the features we've found, we can now draw the sketch. Ensure you include:

1.  **The two branches**: Since $a=7$ (positive), the branches occupy the top-right and bottom-left regions relative to the asymptotes.
2.  **Dashed lines** for the asymptotes at $x = 3$ and $y = 2$.
3.  **Labelled points** for the intercepts at $(0, -\frac{1}{3})$ and $(-\frac{1}{2}, 0)$.

Refer to the image in the markscheme to verify your shape; the graph should never touch or cross the dashed asymptote lines.

parts.content: Expand the expression for $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- $10x - 2x^3 + 10 - 2x^2$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

<note>The expansion may be seen in part 2.</note>

steps.title: Identify the multiplication goal
steps.content: To expand the expression for $f(x)$, we need to multiply the two binomials $(2x + 2)$ and $(5 - x^2)$. Since there are two terms in each set of parentheses, we can use the **Distributive Property**, often called the **FOIL** method (First, Outer, Inner, Last).

steps.title: Apply the FOIL method
steps.content:
We multiply each term in the first parentheses by each term in the second:

*   **First:** $2x \cdot 5 = 10x$
*   **Outer:** $2x \cdot (-x^2) = -2x^3$
*   **Inner:** $2 \cdot 5 = 10$
*   **Last:** $2 \cdot (-x^2) = -2x^2$

steps.title: Combine and state final expression
steps.content:
Now, we combine all the resulting terms into one expression. While the order of terms doesn't change the value, it's common to write them in descending order of the exponent or in the order they were calculated:

$$f(x) = 10x - 2x^3 + 10 - 2x^2$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Plot f1(x) = (2x + 2)(5 - x^2).
3. Plot f2(x) = 10x - 2x^3 + 10 - 2x^2.
4. Check if the graphs are identical.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = (2X + 2)(5 - X²).
3. Enter Y2 = 10X - 2X³ + 10 - 2X².
4. Press [GRAPH]. If the lines overlap, your expansion is correct.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = (2x + 2)(5 - x^2) in the first row.
2. Type y = 10x - 2x^3 + 10 - 2x^2 in the second row.
3. Observe that both functions produce the exact same curve.

calculator.description: You can verify this expansion by graphing the original factored form and your expanded form to see if they overlap perfectly.

steps.title: Set up the grid
steps.content: To expand $(2x + 2)(5 - x^2)$, we use the **Grid Method** (or Box Method). We place the terms of the first binomial $(2x + 2)$ along the left side and the terms of the second binomial $(5 - x^2)$ along the top. $$ \begin{array}{c|c|c} \times & 5 & -x^2 \\ \hline 2x & & \\ \hline +2 & & \end{array} $$

steps.title: Multiply the cells
steps.content:
Now, we multiply the term at the start of each row by the term at the top of each column to fill in the boxes:

1.  $2x \times 5 = 10x$
2.  $2x \times (-x^2) = -2x^3$
3.  $2 \times 5 = 10$
4.  $2 \times (-x^2) = -2x^2$ $$ \begin{array}{c|c|c} \times & 5 & -x^2 \\ \hline 2x & 10x & -2x^3 \\ \hline +2 & 10 & -2x^2 \end{array} $$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * 5 into the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter the multiplication 2 * 5 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * 5 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate simple coefficients for the grid cells.

steps.title: Combine and state answer
steps.content:
To get the final expression, we sum all the values inside the boxes. In IB exams, while you can list them in any order, it's common practice to write them in descending order of the powers of $x$.

$$f(x) = -2x^3 - 2x^2 + 10x + 10$$

According to the markscheme, the answer is accepted in any equivalent form.

parts.content: Find $f'(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- $10 - 6x^2 - 4x$ <marks>A1</marks><marks>A1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award A1 for each correct term. Follow through from part 1. Award at most 2 marks if extra terms are seen.</note>

parts.content: Use your answer to part 2 to find the values of $x$ for which $f$ is increasing.
parts.markscheme:
- Set $f'(x) > 0$: $10 - 6x^2 - 4x > 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $-1.67 < x < 1$ \(\left( -\frac{5}{3} < x < 1 \right)\) <marks>A1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

Award A1 for correct endpoints.
Award A1 for correct inequalities.
Follow through from part 2.</note>

steps.title: Establish the condition
steps.content:
To find where a function $f$ is increasing, we need to determine the interval where its first derivative is positive. This is a fundamental concept in **Topic 5: Calculus (SL 5.2)**.

From the previous part of the question, we found the derivative:
$$f'(x) = 10 - 6x^2 - 4x$$

For $f$ to be increasing, we must satisfy the inequality:
$$f'(x) \gt 0$$
$$10 - 6x^2 - 4x \gt 0$$

highlights.text: Use your answer to part 2 to find the values of x for which f is increasing.

steps.title: Find the critical values
steps.content:
To solve the inequality, we first find the 'roots' (critical values) of the quadratic expression by setting it equal to zero:
$$-6x^2 - 4x + 10 = 0$$

We can simplify this by dividing the entire equation by $-2$:
$$3x^2 + 2x - 5 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula from your **Data Booklet**:
$$x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$

Substituting $a=3, b=2, c=-5$:
$$\begin{aligned} x &= \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{2^2 - 4(3)(-5)}}{2(3)} \\ x &= \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 60}}{6} \\ x &= \frac{-2 \pm 8}{6} \end{aligned}$$

This gives us two roots:
1. $x = \frac{6}{6} = 1$
2. $x = \frac{-10}{6} = -\frac{5}{3} \approx -1.67$

gdc.instructions: Use the polynomial solver (Equation/Solver mode). Enter the coefficients -6, -4, and 10 to find the roots.

ti84.instructions: Go to [APPS], select 'PlySmlt2', then 'Polynomial Root Finder'. Set 'Order' to 2. Enter a2=-6, a1=-4, a0=10. Press [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation y = -6x^2 - 4x + 10 and click on the x-intercepts to find the roots.

calculator.description: Find the roots of the quadratic equation.

steps.title: Solve the inequality
steps.content:
Now we consider the shape of the function $f'(x) = -6x^2 - 4x + 10$. Because the coefficient of $x^2$ is negative ($-6$), the graph of the derivative is a parabola opening downwards.

A downward-opening parabola is positive (above the $x$-axis) **between** its two roots. Therefore, the inequality $f'(x) \gt 0$ is satisfied in the interval:
$$-\frac{5}{3} \lt x \lt 1$$

In decimal form (rounded to 3 significant figures), this is:
$$-1.67 \lt x \lt 1$$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The function $f$ is increasing on the interval where the derivative is positive.

Final answer:
$$-\frac{5}{3} \lt x \lt 1 \text{ or } -1.67 \lt x \lt 1$$

steps.title: Define the Condition
steps.content:
To find where a function $f(x)$ is increasing, we look for the values of $x$ where its gradient is positive. Mathematically, this means the derivative must be greater than zero:

$$f'(x) \gt 0$$

From your work in part 2, you found that the derivative is:

$$f'(x) = 10 - 6x^2 - 4x$$

steps.title: Graph the Derivative
steps.content:
Using the graphical approach, we graph the derivative function $y = 10 - 6x^2 - 4x$ on a GDC. 

Because the coefficient of $x^2$ is negative ($-6$), the graph is a parabola that opens downwards. We are looking for the portion of this graph that lies **above the $x$-axis**, as this represents where $f'(x)$ is positive.

2. Enter the function f1(x) = 10 - 6x² - 4x.
3. Draw the graph.
4. Use the 'G-Solv' or 'Analyze Graph' tool and select 'Root' or 'Zero' to find the x-intercepts.

5. Follow the prompts for 'Left Bound', 'Right Bound', and 'Guess' for both intersections.

2. Click on the gray points where the parabola crosses the x-axis to reveal the coordinates.

calculator.description: Graph the derivative function and find the x-intercepts to identify the region where the graph is positive.

steps.title: Identify the Intercepts
steps.content:
Using the GDC tools, we find the two points where the derivative crosses the $x$-axis ($f'(x) = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= -1.67 \dots \text{ (which is } -\frac{5}{3}) \\ x_2 &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

These values are the "critical points" where the function stops increasing and starts decreasing (or vice versa).

steps.title: Determine the Interval
steps.content:
By looking at the graph on your calculator, you can see that the parabola is above the $x$-axis (positive) **between** the two intercepts. 

Therefore, $f(x)$ is increasing when $x$ is greater than $-1.67$ but less than $1$. We write this using a compound inequality:

$$-1.67 \lt x \lt 1$$

You can also provide the exact fractional form if required:

$$-\frac{5}{3} \lt x \lt 1$$

steps.title: Define an Increasing Function
steps.content:
To find where the function $f(x)$ is increasing, we look for the interval of $x$-values where the graph goes "upwards" from left to right. Mathematically, this occurs when the gradient (or derivative) is positive:

$$f'(x) \gt 0$$

Since we are using the **GDC Analysis of the Original Function**, we will identify the turning points (local minimum and local maximum) on the graph of $f(x)$ to find this region.

steps.title: Graph the Function
steps.content:
First, enter the function into your graphing calculator:

$$f(x) = (2x + 2)(5 - x^2)$$ 

Adjust your window settings so you can clearly see the "humps" of the cubic graph. A standard window like $x: [-5, 5]$ and $y: [-10, 20]$ should work well here.

gdc.instructions: Go to Graph mode, enter y1 = (2x+2)(5-x^2). Press Draw/Graph.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=], enter (2X+2)(5-X^2) in Y1. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type f(x) = (2x+2)(5-x^2) into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Graph the function to visualize turning points.

steps.title: Find Turning Points
steps.content:
A function increases between its local minimum and its local maximum. We use the GDC tools to find these $x$-coordinates:

1.  **Local Minimum**: Locate the bottom of the "valley". The calculator identifies this at $x \approx -1.67$.
2.  **Local Maximum**: Locate the top of the "hill". The calculator identifies this at $x = 1$.

These points mark the boundaries where the function changes from decreasing to increasing, and then back to decreasing.

gdc.instructions: Press [F5] (G-Solv). Press [F3] for MIN and [F2] for MAX.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC). Select '3: minimum', choose left and right bounds. Repeat using '4: maximum'.

desmos.instructions: Click directly on the low point and high point of the curve to see their coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the local minimum and maximum coordinates.

steps.title: Determine the Interval
steps.content:
By looking at the graph, we see that $f(x)$ starts low at the minimum point and goes up until it reaches the maximum point. Therefore, the function is increasing for all $x$-values between these two points.

From our GDC results:
$$x_{min} \approx -1.67$$
$$x_{max} = 1$$

In IB notation, we express this as an inequality:
$$-1.67 \lt x \lt 1$$

steps.title: Final Answer and Marks
steps.content:
To ensure full marks, remember that while you used a graphical method, the condition for a function to be increasing is that its derivative is positive:

$$f'(x) \gt 0$$

Based on your previous work or the graphical analysis, the solution is:
$$-1.67 \lt x \lt 1$$

(Note: $-1.67$ is the 3 significant figure approximation of $-\frac{5}{3}$).

**Mark Distribution:**
- **M1**: For identifying that the function increases when $f'(x) \gt 0$.
- **A1**: For the correct lower endpoint ($-1.67$).
- **A1**: For the correct upper endpoint ($1$) and correct inequality symbols.

parts.content: Find the **exact** value of each of the zeros of $f$.
parts.markscheme:
- $-1, \sqrt{5}, -\sqrt{5}$ <marks>A1</marks><marks>A1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award A1 for -1 and each correct exact value.</note>

steps.title: Set the function to zero
steps.content:
To find the zeros of a function, we need to find the values of $x$ for which $f(x) = 0$. Using the function given in the question:

$$(2x + 2)(5 - x^2) = 0$$

steps.title: Apply Zero Product Property
steps.content:
The **Zero Product Property** states that if the product of two factors is zero, then at least one of the factors must be zero. This allows us to split our equation into two simpler parts:

1.  $2x + 2 = 0$
2.  $5 - x^2 = 0$

steps.title: Solve the linear factor
steps.content:
First, we solve the linear equation for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + 2 &= 0 \\ 2x &= -2 \\ x &= -1 \end{aligned}$$

This gives us our first zero.

gdc.instructions: Use the Solve function: Solve(2x + 2 = 0, x)

ti84.instructions: Go to the Solver (MATH > B: Solver). Enter 0 = 2X + 2 and solve for X.

desmos.instructions: Graph y = 2x + 2 and find where the line crosses the x-axis.

calculator.description: Verify the solution for the linear factor.

steps.title: Solve the quadratic factor
steps.content: Next, we solve the quadratic equation for $x$: $$ \begin{aligned} 5 - x^2 &= 0 \\ x^2 &= 5 \\ x &= \pm\sqrt{5} \end{aligned} $$ Since the question asks for **exact** values, we must leave the answers in surd form rather than using decimals.

gdc.instructions: Graph y = 5 - x^2 and use the G-Solv tool to find roots.

ti84.instructions: Graph Y1 = 5 - X² and use '2nd > CALC > 2: zero' to find the x-intercepts.

desmos.instructions: Graph y = 5 - x^2 and click on the x-intercepts to see the values.

calculator.description: Visualize the zeros of the quadratic factor.

steps.title: State the final zeros
steps.content:
Combining the results from both factors, the exact zeros of $f$ are:

$$x = -1, \quad x = \sqrt{5}, \quad x = -\sqrt{5}$$

In an IB exam, each correct exact value would earn you 1 mark.

steps.title: Expand the function
steps.content:
To find the zeros using expansion, we first multiply the two factors $(2x + 2)$ and $(5 - x^2)$ using the FOIL method (First, Outer, Inner, Last).

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= (2x + 2)(5 - x^2) \\ &= 2x(5) + 2x(-x^2) + 2(5) + 2(-x^2) \\ &= 10x - 2x^3 + 10 - 2x^2 \end{aligned}$$

Rearranging the terms into standard cubic form gives us:
$$f(x) = -2x^3 - 2x^2 + 10x + 10$$

steps.title: Set the function to zero
steps.content:
The zeros of a function are the $x$-values where $f(x) = 0$. Using our expanded form, we set up the following equation:

$$-2x^3 - 2x^2 + 10x + 10 = 0$$

To simplify the calculation, we can divide the entire equation by $-2$:

$$x^3 + x^2 - 5x - 5 = 0$$

steps.title: Factor by grouping
steps.content:
Now we factor the polynomial by grouping the terms into two pairs:

$$(x^3 + x^2) + (-5x - 5) = 0$$

Factor out the greatest common factor from each pair:

$$x^2(x + 1) - 5(x + 1) = 0$$

Since $(x + 1)$ is common to both terms, we can factor it out:

$$(x^2 - 5)(x + 1) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
Using the Null Factor Law, we set each factor equal to zero and solve for $x$:

1. For the first factor: $$ \begin{aligned} x + 1 &= 0 \\ x &= -1 \end{aligned} $$
2. For the second factor: $$ \begin{aligned} x^2 - 5 &= 0 \\ x^2 &= 5 \\ x &= \pm\sqrt{5} \end{aligned} $$

The question asks for **exact** values, so we leave the results as square roots rather than decimals.

steps.title: Verify with a calculator
steps.content: You can verify these zeros by graphing $f(x)$ on your GDC and finding the x-intercepts, or by using the polynomial solver tool.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Equation/Polynomial solver menu.
2. Choose 'Polynomial' and degree 3.
3. Enter the coefficients: 1, 1, -5, -5.
4. Solve to see the roots.

2. Select 'Polynomial Root Finder'.
3. Set Order to 3 and press [NEXT] (graph button).
4. Enter the coefficients: a3=1, a2=1, a1=-5, a0=-5.
5. Press [SOLVE] (graph button).

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The exact zeros of the function are:

$$x = -1, \quad x = \sqrt{5}, \quad x = -\sqrt{5}$$

parts.content: Write down the coordinates of the point of intersection.

<note>Award A1 for 1.49 and A1 for 13.9. Accept $x = 1.49$ and $y = 13.9$. Follow through from part 1.</note>

steps.title: Define the intersection point
steps.content:
Hey there! To find the point of intersection between two functions, $f(x)$ and $g(x)$, we are essentially looking for the $(x, y)$ coordinate where the two graphs cross. At this point, the $y$-values of both functions are equal. 

We are given:
$$f(x) = (2x + 2)(5 - x^2)$$
$$g(x) = 5^x + 6x - 6$$

highlights.text: coordinates of the point of intersection

steps.title: Input functions into GDC
steps.content: The most efficient way to solve this in an IB Math AI exam is to use your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC). Start by entering both equations into the graphing menu as separate functions.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graph menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = (2x + 2)(5 - x^2).
3. Enter f2(x) = 5^x + 6x - 6.
4. Press [DRAW] or [EXE].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = (2X + 2)(5 - X²).
3. Enter Y2 = 5^X + 6X - 6.
4. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type f(x) = (2x + 2)(5 - x^2) in the first expression bar.
2. Type g(x) = 5^x + 6x - 6 in the second expression bar.

calculator.description: Graphing both functions to find their intersection.

steps.title: Adjust the viewing window
steps.content: If you can't see where the curves cross, you may need to adjust your window settings. Based on the functions, a window where $x$ goes from $-5$ to $5$ and $y$ goes from $-10$ to $20$ should allow you to see the intersection point clearly in the first quadrant.

steps.title: Use the intersection tool
steps.content: Now, use the calculator's built-in tool to calculate the exact coordinates. The GDC will provide a high-precision result, which we will then round.

gdc.instructions:
1. Press [F5] (G-Solv).
2. Select [F5] (ISCT) for Intersection.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC).
2. Select '5: intersect'.
3. Press [ENTER] for 'First curve?', 'Second curve?', and 'Guess?'.

desmos.instructions: Click on the point where the two graphs intersect to display the coordinates.

calculator.description: Using the calculation tool to find the intersection.

steps.title: State the final coordinates
steps.content:
Your calculator should give you the values $x \approx 1.48702...$ and $y \approx 13.8714...$. 

In IB exams, unless stated otherwise, we always round our final answers to **3 significant figures**. 

- For $x = 1.48702...$, the third significant figure is $8$. Since the next digit is $7$, we round up to $1.49$.
- For $y = 13.8714...$, the third significant figure is $8$. Since the next digit is $7$, we round up to $13.9$.

Thus, the coordinates are:
$$(1.49, 13.9)$$ 

You earn 1 mark for the correct $x$-coordinate and 1 mark for the correct $y$-coordinate.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
To find where the graphs of $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ intersect, we need to find the $x$-value where the two functions are equal. We set them up as an equation:

$$(2x + 2)(5 - x^2) = 5^x + 6x - 6$$

steps.title: Use the GDC solver
steps.content:
Instead of solving this algebraically (which would be very difficult with the $5^x$ term!), we use the **Numeric Solver** on the GDC. Enter both sides of the equation and solve for $x$. 

From the GDC, we find:
$$x \approx 1.48702...$$

gdc.instructions: Go to the Equation menu and select 'Solver'. Enter the equation (2x+2)(5-x^2) = 5^x + 6x-6. Set a starting guess for x and press 'Solve'.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH], then select 'B: Solver...' or 'Numeric Solver'. Enter Equation 1 as (2X+2)(5-X^2) and Equation 2 as 5^X+6X-6. Provide an initial guess for X (like 1.5) and press [ALPHA][SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into a cell or graph both functions and click on the intersection point.

calculator.description: Solve the equation for x using the numeric solver.

steps.title: Calculate the y-coordinate
steps.content:
Now that we have the $x$-coordinate, substitute it back into either $f(x)$ or $g(x)$ to find the corresponding $y$-coordinate. Using $f(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= (2(1.48702...) + 2)(5 - (1.48702...)^2) \\ y &\approx 13.8714... \end{aligned}$$

This is a great way to double-check your work; if you plug it into $g(x)$, you should get the same result!

steps.title: State final coordinates
steps.content:
Finally, we state the answer as coordinates $(x, y)$. Remember that IB exams usually require answers to be rounded to **3 significant figures** unless specified otherwise.

$$x \approx 1.49, \quad y \approx 13.9$$

The coordinates are $(1.49, 13.9)$. 

This uses the modeling skills found in **Topic 2: Functions** of your syllabus, specifically finding intersection points of different functions.

parts.content: **Draw** the graph of $f$ for $-3 \leqslant x \leqslant 3$ and $-40 \leqslant y \leqslant 20$. Use a scale of 2 cm to represent 1 unit on the $x$-axis and 1 cm to represent 5 units on the $y$-axis.

steps.title: Create a table of values
steps.content:
To draw an accurate graph, we first calculate the $y$-coordinates for specific $x$-values across the domain $-3 \leqslant x \leqslant 3$. Since we are plotting by hand, using integer steps is the most efficient way to start.

We substitute these values into the function:
$$f(x) = (2x + 2)(5 - x^2)$$

| $x$ | Calculation | $y$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $-3$ | $(2(-3)+2)(5-(-3)^2) = (-4)(-4)$ | $16$ |
| $-2$ | $(2(-2)+2)(5-(-2)^2) = (-2)(1)$ | $-2$ |
| $-1$ | $(2(-1)+2)(5-(-1)^2) = (0)(4)$ | $0$ |
| $0$ | $(2(0)+2)(5-0^2) = (2)(5)$ | $10$ |
| $1$ | $(2(1)+2)(5-1^2) = (4)(4)$ | $16$ |
| $2$ | $(2(2)+2)(5-2^2) = (6)(1)$ | $6$ |
| $3$ | $(2(3)+2)(5-3^2) = (8)(-4)$ | $-32$ |

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Table menu.
2. Enter the function f(x) = (2x + 2)(5 - x^2).
3. Set the range from -3 to 3 with a step of 1.
4. View the table of coordinates.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = (2X + 2)(5 - X²).
2. Press [2nd] [WINDOW] (TBLSET) and set TblStart = -3 and ΔTbl = 1.
3. Press [2nd] [GRAPH] (TABLE) to view the coordinates.

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the 'plus' icon and select 'Table'.
2. Enter the x-values from -3 to 3.
3. In the y1 column header, type (2x1 + 2)(5 - x1^2).

calculator.description: Calculate y-values using a table

steps.title: Set up the axes
steps.content:
Before plotting, prepare your grid according to the specific scale requested in the question. This is a common requirement in IB Math AI exams to test your precision.

*   **$x$-axis:** Use a scale of 2 cm to represent 1 unit. This means if your grid has 1 cm squares, each number on the axis will be 2 squares apart.
*   **$y$-axis:** Use a scale of 1 cm to represent 5 units. Every 1 cm square vertical jump represents a value change of 5.

Label your axes $x$ and $y$ and draw them clearly with a ruler.

highlights.text: Use a scale of 2 cm to represent 1 unit on the $x$-axis and 1 cm to represent 5 units on the $y$-axis.

steps.title: Identify key features
steps.content:
To get full marks for the shape of the curve, it helps to find the $x$-intercepts (roots). We find these by setting $f(x) = 0$:

1.  $2x + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow x = -1$
2.  $5 - x^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x^2 = 5 \Rightarrow x = \pm \sqrt{5} \approx \pm 2.24$

Plotting these points ($(-2.24, 0)$, $(-1, 0)$, and $(2.24, 0)$) ensures your intercepts are in the correct locations as required by the markscheme.

gdc.instructions:
1. Graph the function.
2. Use the 'G-Solv' or 'Analyze Graph' tool and select 'Root' or 'Zero'.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '2: zero'.
2. Follow the prompts for 'Left Bound', 'Right Bound', and 'Guess'.

desmos.instructions:
1. Graph the function.
2. Click on the gray dots where the graph crosses the x-axis to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find zeros/intercepts

steps.title: Plot and draw curve
steps.content:
Now, perform the final plotting:

1.  **Plot the points** from your table: $(-3, 16)$, $(-2, -2)$, $(-1, 0)$, $(0, 10)$, $(1, 16)$, $(2, 6)$, and $(3, -32)$.
2.  **Add the intercepts** found in the previous step.
3.  **Connect the points** with a single, smooth, continuous curve. Avoid using a ruler between points; the curve should be rounded at the peaks and troughs.

Ensure your curve starts exactly at $x = -3$ and ends exactly at $x = 3$.

highlights.text: Smooth continuous curve with approximate correct shape in the given domain

steps.title: Set up your GDC
steps.content: To get an accurate sketch, we first need to input the function into your graphing calculator and set the correct viewing window. This ensures we see the entire graph within the domain and range required by the question.

3. Press [GRAPH].

2. Click the wrench icon (Settings) and set x-axis from -3 to 3 and y-axis from -40 to 20.

steps.title: Identify intercepts
steps.content:
Using the GDC's built-in features, find where the graph crosses the axes. These are essential 'anchor points' for your drawing.

- **$x$-intercepts**: Find the roots (zeros).
- **$y$-intercept**: Find the value when $x = 0$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Press [F5] (G-Solve) then [F1] (ROOT) to find x-intercepts.
2. Press [F5] (G-Solve) then [F4] (Y-ICEPT) to find the y-intercept.

2. Press [2nd] [TRACE] then [1: value] and enter X = 0 to find the y-intercept.

calculator.description: Find the intercepts using the calculator analysis tools.

steps.title: Find local extrema
steps.content:
Next, find the coordinates of the turning points. These are the highest and lowest points within the specific regions of the graph.

From your GDC, you should find:
- **Local Minimum**: approximately $(-1.67, -2.96)$
- **Local Maximum**: $(1, 16)$

gdc.instructions:

2. Press [F5] (G-Solve) then [F3] (MIN) for the minimum.

2. Press [2nd] [TRACE] then [4: maximum].

desmos.instructions: 1. Click on the gray dots at the peaks and valleys of the curve to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the maximum and minimum points.

steps.title: Calculate boundary endpoints
steps.content:
Since the question asks for the graph specifically between $x = -3$ and $x = 3$, we must calculate the exact coordinates at these boundaries.

$$\begin{aligned} f(-3) &= (2(-3) + 2)(5 - (-3)^2) = (-4)(-4) = 16 \\ f(3) &= (2(3) + 2)(5 - (3)^2) = (8)(-4) = -32 \end{aligned}$$

So, your graph should start at $(-3, 16)$ and end at $(3, -32)$.

highlights.text: Draw the graph of f for -3 <= x <= 3

steps.title: Plot and sketch
steps.content:
Now, translate these points onto your paper using the required scale:
- **$x$-axis**: 1 unit = 2 cm
- **$y$-axis**: 5 units = 1 cm

**Steps for the 4 marks:**
1. Draw and label your axes using a ruler.
2. Mark the $x$-intercepts at $(-2.24, 0)$, $(-1, 0)$, and $(2.24, 0)$.
3. Mark the $y$-intercept at $(0, 10)$.
4. Mark the extrema at $(-1.67, -2.96)$ and $(1, 16)$.
5. Connect the points with a **smooth, continuous curve**.

parts.markscheme:
• Attempt to substitute $x=1$: $f(1) = 1^2+1+\frac{50}{1}$ M1
  • $52$ A1

steps.title: Understand function notation
steps.content:
To find $f(1)$ , we use the method of **direct substitution**. This means we replace every instance of the variable $x$ in the function formula with the number $1$ .

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic 2: Functions** (specifically SL 2.2: Finding values of a function).

steps.title: Substitute x = 1
steps.content:
Substitute $1$ into the expression for $f(x)$ :

$$f(1) = (1)^2 + (1) + \frac{50}{(1)}$$

In the IB markscheme, showing this substitution step is usually worth a method mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Now, we perform the arithmetic step-by-step:

$$1^2 = 1$$
$$\frac{50}{1} = 50$$

Adding these together:

$$f(1) = 1 + 1 + 50$$

gdc.instructions: Type the expression into the main calculation window.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression in an empty cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the final value of the function using direct substitution.

steps.title: State final result
steps.content:
Summing the terms gives us the final answer:

$$f(1) = 52$$

Providing the correct numerical value earns the final answer mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Analyze the goal
steps.content: To find $f(1)$ , we need to evaluate the function when the input $x$ is equal to $1$ . This is a fundamental skill in **Topic 2: Functions** of your syllabus. Using a graphing calculator (GDC) makes this process very efficient.

steps.title: Input the function
steps.content:
The first step is to enter the function into your calculator's function editor (usually the **Y=** menu). 

$$y_1 = x^2 + x + \frac{50}{x}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the function into the expression bar.

steps.title: Evaluate at x = 1
steps.content:
Using the GDC's evaluation tools, we can find the $y$ -coordinate when $x = 1$ .

On most calculators, you can use the **Table** tool or the **Value** function on a graph. Even though we are using technology, the IB markscheme expects you to show the substitution to earn the first mark:

$$f(1) = 1^2 + 1 + \frac{50}{1}$$

steps.content:
After substituting or using your calculator's table, you will see that the result is $52$. 

$$f(1) = 52$$

steps.title: Define the objective
steps.content:
To solve $f(x) = 0$ , we are looking for the $x$ -intercept of the graph of the function. In mathematical terms, we want to find the **roots** or **zeros** of the equation:

$$x^2 + x + \frac{50}{x} = 0$$

Since this is a non-linear equation, the most efficient way to solve it in the IB Math Applications and Interpretation (AI) course is by using a Graphing Display Calculator (GDC).

steps.title: Enter the function
steps.content:
First, enter the function into your graphing tool.

Let: $$y_1 = x^2 + x + \frac{50}{x}$$

When you graph this, you will notice a break in the curve at $x = 0$ because the function is undefined there. Look for where the curve crosses the horizontal $x$ -axis.

2. Type x^2 + x + 50/x into Y1.
3. Press [F6] to Draw.

3. Press [GRAPH].

steps.title: Identify the root
steps.content: Use the built-in calculation tools to find the exact coordinates of the $x$-intercept. The calculator will identify the value of $x$ when $y = 0$.

2. Press [F1] (ROOT).

2. Select '2: zero'.

steps.content:
The calculator displays a value of approximately $-4.04932 \dots$ .

Following the IB standard of rounding to **3 significant figures**, we get:

$$x = -4.05$$

steps.title: Identify the equation
steps.content:
To solve $f(x) = 0$, we need to find the value of $x$ that makes the function equal to zero. We set up the following equation:

$$x^2 + x + \frac{50}{x} = 0$$

Since this is a non-linear equation, the most efficient way to solve it in the Math AI course is by using the **Numerical Solver** on your graphing calculator.

steps.title: Use the Numerical Solver
steps.content:
Using the numerical solver tool, we enter the equation exactly as it appears. 

This falls under **Topic 1: Number and Algebra** (specifically SL 1.8), which encourages the use of technology to solve complex equations that aren't easily solved by hand.

2. In E1, enter: x² + x + 50/x
3. In E2, enter: 0

5. Enter an initial guess (e.g., x = -5) and press [ALPHA][SOLVE].

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator provides the solution:
$$x \approx -4.04932...$$

In the IB Diploma Programme, unless otherwise stated, all numerical answers should be given to **three significant figures**.

$$x = -4.05$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
To solve $f(x) = 0$, we start by setting the given function expression equal to zero:

$$x^2 + x + \frac{50}{x} = 0$$

steps.title: Convert to polynomial form
steps.content:
To use the **Polynomial Root Finder** on your GDC, we need the equation in standard polynomial form ($ax^n + bx^{n-1} + \dots = 0$). 

We can eliminate the fraction by multiplying every term by $x$ :

$$x(x^2 + x + \frac{50}{x}) = x(0)$$
$$x^3 + x^2 + 50 = 0$$

This is now a cubic equation (a polynomial of degree 3). This technique is part of **Topic 1.8: Use of technology to solve polynomial equations**.

steps.title: Use the GDC
steps.content:
Now we identify the coefficients for the cubic equation $ax^3 + bx^2 + cx + d = 0$:
*   $a = 1$
*   $b = 1$
*   $c = 0$ (since there is no $x$ term)
*   $d = 50$

Enter these into your calculator's polynomial solver.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the 'Equation' or 'Poly Root Finder' menu.
2. Choose 'Polynomial' and set the degree to 3.
3. Input the coefficients: 1, 1, 0, 50.
4. Press 'Solve' or 'EXE'.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [APPS] and select 'PlySmlt2'.
2. Select 'Polynomial Root Finder'.
3. Set 'Order' to 3 and press [NEXT].
4. Enter coefficients: a3=1, a2=1, a1=0, a0=50.
5. Press [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Graph the function y = x³ + x² + 50 and find the x-intercept.

calculator.description: Solve the cubic equation x³ + x² + 50 = 0 using the polynomial root finder tool.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The calculator provides the real root as approximately $-4.04932...$

In IB exams, unless otherwise stated, answers should be rounded to **three significant figures**.

$$x \approx -4.05$$

parts.content:
The graph of $f$ has a local minimum at point $\text{A}$.

Find the coordinates of $\text{A}$.
parts.markscheme:
• $(2.76649\dots, \, 28.4934\dots)$
  • $\text{A}(2.77, \, 28.5)$ A1 A1

2 marks total

steps.title: Rewrite the function
steps.content:
To make differentiation easier using the power rule, let's rewrite the term $\frac{50}{x}$ using a negative exponent:

$$f(x) = x^2 + x + 50x^{-1}$$

steps.title: Find the derivative
steps.content:
Using the power rule from the data booklet, we differentiate each term of $f(x)$:

$$f'(x) = 2x + 1 - 50x^{-2}$$

Which can be written as:

$$f'(x) = 2x + 1 - \frac{50}{x^2}$$

steps.title: Find the x-coordinate
steps.content:
At a local minimum, the gradient (derivative) is zero. Set $f'(x) = 0$ and solve for $x$ :

$$2x + 1 - \frac{50}{x^2} = 0$$

Multiplying by $x^2$ gives a cubic equation:

$$2x^3 + x^2 - 50 = 0$$

Using a calculator to solve this equation, we find:

$$x \approx 2.76649 \dots$$

gdc.instructions: Use the polynomial solver (Equation) mode. Select Polynomial, Degree 3. Enter coefficients: a=2, b=1, c=0, d=-50. Solve for x.

ti84.instructions: Press [MATH], scroll to [Solver...] or [Numeric Solver]. Enter the equation: 0 = 2X^3 + X^2 - 50. Press [ALPHA] then [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions: Graph y = 2x^3 + x^2 - 50 and find the x-intercept.

calculator.description: Solving the equation $2x^3 + x^2 - 50 = 0$ using a solver tool.

steps.title: Find the y-coordinate
steps.content:
Substitute the $x$ value back into the original function $f(x)$ to find the $y$ -coordinate of point $A$ :

$$f(2.76649 \dots) = (2.76649 \dots)^2 + 2.76649 \dots + \frac{50}{2.76649 \dots}$$
$$y \approx 28.4934 \dots$$

gdc.instructions: Perform the calculation on the main run screen.

ti84.instructions: In the main screen, type the expression using the stored X value: X^2 + X + 50/X.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new line.

calculator.description: Evaluate the original function at the critical point.

steps.title: State the coordinates
steps.content:
Rounding both coordinates to 3 significant figures as per standard IB practice:

$$x \approx 2.77$$
$$y \approx 28.5$$

The coordinates of point $A$ are $(2.77, 28.5)$ .

steps.title: Enter the function
steps.content:
To find the coordinates of the local minimum point $A$, we first need to input the function into our graphing calculator. 

Enter the following expression into the function editor (usually the **Y=** menu):

$$f(x) = x^2 + x + \frac{50}{x}$$

This function is covered in **Topic 5: Calculus** regarding stationary points. Since we are using a GDC approach, we don't need to differentiate manually; we will let the calculator find the lowest point in that region for us.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Graphing menu. Define f1(x) = x^2 + x + 50/x.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=]. Enter X^2 + X + 50 / X into Y1.

desmos.instructions: Type the function into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Enter the function into the Y= menu.

steps.title: Graph and adjust window
steps.content:
Press the **Graph** button. Depending on your current settings, you might not see the local minimum clearly. We need to adjust the window to focus on the positive $x$ region where the curve turns back up.

A good window for this function is:
- $Xmin = 0$
- $Xmax = 10$
- $Ymin = 0$
- $Ymax = 50$

You should see a curve that drops down from the left and then starts rising again; the lowest point of this 'valley' is point $A$ .

gdc.instructions: Go to Menu > Window/Zoom > Window Settings. Set Xmin=0, Xmax=10, Ymin=0, Ymax=50.

ti84.instructions: Press [WINDOW]. Set Xmin=0, Xmax=10, Ymin=0, Ymax=50. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Click the wrench icon (Graph Settings) and set the X-axis from 0 to 10 and the Y-axis from 0 to 50.

calculator.description: Adjust the viewing window to see the minimum clearly.

steps.title: Find the local minimum
steps.content:
Now, use the calculator's built-in tool to identify the exact coordinates of the local minimum.

The calculator will ask for a **Left Bound** (move the cursor to the left of the minimum), a **Right Bound** (move it to the right), and a **Guess** (place it near the bottom). 

The calculator provides the coordinates:

$$x \approx 2.76649 \dots$$
$$y \approx 28.4934 \dots$$

gdc.instructions: Press Menu > Analyze Graph > Minimum. Click to the left and then to the right of the minimum point.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC). Select '3: minimum'. Follow prompts for Left Bound, Right Bound, and Guess.

desmos.instructions: Click on the curve. A grey dot will appear at the minimum. Click the dot to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Calculate the local minimum coordinates.

steps.title: State the final coordinates
steps.content:
In IB exams, unless otherwise stated, you should round your final answers to **3 significant figures**.

- The $x$ coordinate $2.76649 \dots$ rounds to $2.77$ .
- The $y$ coordinate $28.4934 \dots$ rounds to $28.5$ .

Therefore, the coordinates of point $A$ are:

$$A(2.77, 28.5)$$

parts.content: Find the vertex of the function and interpret it as the maximum height reached by the ball or the minimum.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to complete the square or use the vertex formula <marks>M1</marks>

- State the coordinates of the vertex: $(2, 4)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Interpret as a maximum point, representing a maximum height of 4 meters at 2 seconds <marks>A1</marks>

**Method 1 (Completing Square)**
- Rewrite function: $f(x) = -(x^2 - 4x)$
- Complete the square: $-(x^2 - 4x + 4 - 4)$
- Simplify: $-(x-2)^2 + 4$
- Identify vertex: $(2, 4)$

**Method 2 (Vertex Formula)**
- Use formula $x = -\frac{b}{2a}$ with $a=-1, b=4$
- Calculate $x$: $x = -\frac{4}{2(-1)} = 2$
- Substitute $x=2$ into function: $f(2) = -(2)^2 + 4(2) = -4 + 8 = 4$
- Identify vertex: $(2, 4)$

parts.content: Identify the axis of symmetry for the function.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize that the axis of symmetry is the vertical line passing through the vertex <marks>M1</marks>

- State the equation of the axis of symmetry: $x = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the coefficients
steps.content:
To find the axis of symmetry, we first need to identify the coefficients $a$ and $b$ from the quadratic function $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$. 

A quadratic function in standard form is written as $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$. 

By comparing this to our function, we can see:
- $a = -1$ (the coefficient of $x^2$)
- $b = 4$ (the coefficient of $x$)

steps.title: Reference the formula
steps.content:
In your IB formula booklet, under the **Functions** section, you are given a specific formula to calculate the axis of symmetry for any quadratic function. 

$$\text{Quadratic axis of symmetry: } x = -\frac{b}{2a}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, substitute our values $a = -1$ and $b = 4$ into the formula to find the value of $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= -\frac{4}{2(-1)} \\ x &= -\frac{4}{-2} \\ x &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns you the first mark (**M1**) for correctly applying the method.

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The axis of symmetry is always written as an equation of a vertical line. 

Therefore, the axis of symmetry is:
$$x = 2$$

This final statement earns the second mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: You can verify this result on your GDC by finding the vertex of the graph. The $x$-coordinate of the vertex is always the same as the axis of symmetry.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu and enter the function y = -x² + 4x.
2. Use the 'G-Solv' (F5) tool and select 'MAX' (F2).
3. The calculator will provide the coordinates of the vertex (2, 4).

1. Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = -X² + 4X.
2. Press [GRAPH].
3. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '4: maximum'.
4. Set Left Bound, Right Bound, and Guess.
5. The x-value displayed is the axis of symmetry.

2. Click on the gray point at the peak of the parabola.
3. The x-coordinate of the point (2, 4) is your axis of symmetry.

steps.title: Set function to zero
steps.content:
To find the axis of symmetry using the roots, we first need to determine where the graph crosses the $x$-axis (the ground level). We do this by setting the height function $f(x)$ to zero.

$$-x^2 + 4x = 0$$

steps.title: Factor to find roots
steps.content:
Next, we solve for $x$ by factoring the quadratic expression. We can factor out a common term of $x$:

$$x(-x + 4) = 0$$

Setting each factor to zero gives us our two roots (the $x$-intercepts):
1.  $x = 0$
2.  $-x + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 4$

These represent the times when the ball is on the ground: at the start ($0$ seconds) and when it lands ($4$ seconds).

steps.title: Average the roots
steps.content:
A parabola is perfectly symmetrical. The axis of symmetry is the vertical line that passes exactly through the midpoint of the two roots. We calculate this by finding the average of $x = 0$ and $x = 4$:

$$x = \frac{0 + 4}{2}$$

$$x = 2$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the mean of the two roots using the basic division function.

ti84.instructions: Enter (0 + 4) / 2 on the home screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell to find the average.

calculator.description: Calculate the midpoint of the roots 0 and 4.

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The axis of symmetry must be written as an equation of a vertical line. Based on our calculation, the axis of symmetry is:

$$x = 2$$

This aligns with the markscheme requirement to state the full equation for the final mark.

highlights.text: State the equation of the axis of symmetry: x = 2

steps.title: Understand the vertex and symmetry
steps.content: Hi there! To find the axis of symmetry using calculus, we need to locate the $x$-coordinate of the vertex. Since the vertex is the maximum point of this parabola, the gradient (slope) of the function at that point will be exactly zero. This means we are looking for the point where the first derivative $f'(x) = 0$.

steps.title: Differentiate the height function
steps.content:
We start with the function for height:

$$f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$$

Using the **power rule** from the Calculus section of your data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} f'(x) &= \frac{d}{dx}(-x^2) + \frac{d}{dx}(4x) \\ f'(x) &= -2x + 4 \end{aligned}$$

This derivative represents the rate of change of height over time.

steps.title: Solve for the stationary point
steps.content:
The axis of symmetry occurs at the $x$-value where the gradient is zero. We set our derivative equal to zero and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= -2x + 4 \\ 2x &= 4 \\ x &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

The $x$-coordinate of the vertex is $2$ seconds.

2. Use the 'G-Solv' or 'Analyze Graph' tool to find the 'MAX'.

steps.title: State the axis of symmetry
steps.content:
The axis of symmetry is always written as the equation of a vertical line. Based on our calculation, the axis of symmetry is:

$$x = 2$$

In the IB markscheme, you earn **M1** for recognizing the relationship between the vertex and the axis of symmetry, and **A1** for providing the correct final equation.

highlights.text: State the equation of the axis of symmetry: $x = 2$

steps.title: Understand the Axis of Symmetry
steps.content:
The axis of symmetry of a parabola is the vertical line that passes directly through the vertex (the maximum or minimum point). It essentially acts as a mirror, splitting the graph into two identical halves.

Since the height function $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$ has a negative coefficient for $x^2$, we know the parabola opens downwards and the vertex will be a maximum point.

steps.title: Graph the Function
steps.content:
To find the axis of symmetry graphically, we first need to input the function into our Graphing Display Calculator (GDC). 

Enter the equation into the graphing menu:
$$y_1 = -x^2 + 4x$$

Adjust your window settings so you can clearly see the peak of the curve. A good window for this function would be $x$ from $[0, 5]$ and $y$ from $[0, 5]$.

steps.content:
Using the GDC's built-in tools, we can find the exact coordinates of the vertex by locating the **maximum** point of the curve. 

When you run the 'Maximum' analysis, the calculator will identify the peak at:
$$(2, 4)$$

The $x$-coordinate of this point tells us where the vertical line of symmetry is located.

steps.title: Verify Using a Table
steps.content:
You can also verify this by looking at a table of values. If the axis of symmetry is at $x = 2$, values of $f(x)$ should be the same for points equidistant from $x = 2$.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline x & f(x) \\ \hline 1 & 3 \\ \hline 2 & 4 \\ \hline 3 & 3 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Notice how at $x=1$ and $x=3$ (both one unit away from $x=2$), the height is exactly the same ($3$ meters). This confirms that $x=2$ is the center of the graph.

steps.title: State the Final Equation
steps.content:
The axis of symmetry is always written as an equation of a vertical line: $x = k$. Since our vertex has an $x$-coordinate of $2$, the equation is:

$$x = 2$$

In an IB exam, simply writing "2" might not earn full marks; you must provide the full equation for the line.

The $y$-coordinate of the minimum of the graph is $3$.
